content: Stop trying to initialize your services by calling them directly from the main function or using a global singleton; that approach breaks testability and forces you to refactor everything when you need to mock an external API. The correct pattern for Rust is constructor injection, where every service owns its dependencies as explicit fields in its struct definition. When designing your `PaymentService` struct, place the `HttpClient` and `DatabasePool` instances directly inside it so that the service cannot function without them. This simple structural change makes your code immediately testable because you can swap out those fields with mocks in isolation without touching business logic. Instead of writing a unit test where you manually instantiate real HTTP clients which are slow to start up, you construct a `PaymentService` using a `MockHttpClient` and a `FakeDatabasePool`, allowing you to verify behavior deterministically at the speed your tests require. You do not need a heavy dependency injection framework or runtime reflection to achieve this; Rust's compile-time type system already guarantees that every field is initialized correctly, which eliminates the entire category of runtime initialization errors. The shaku crate provides a pragmatic way to implement this pattern without writing boilerplate by offering traits like `Dependency` and `Provide` that let you define custom injection mechanisms while still letting you write standard Rust code. When using shaku or any DI library, remember that your goal is not to automate every aspect of dependency management but to provide the scaffolding for manual construction in test scenarios. The most common mistake developers make is implementing a framework-only approach where they forget to actually instantiate the dependencies manually during testing; always ensure you can write code that constructs services using `std::mem::replace` or explicit constructors when running integration tests against real environments. If you follow this strict constructor injection pattern, your `PaymentService` will accept only types that implement the necessary traits for serialization and deserialization, making it safe to serialize entire application states across processes without manual intervention. This approach also naturally prevents circular dependencies because the compiler will catch them at compile time before you even run a test suite, whereas lazy initialization patterns often hide these issues until runtime failure.
